top_stories.label: Top Stories
top_stories.ai_summary: Conflict and diplomacy share the headlines. Ukraine struck deep inside Russia overnight — hitting a 'shadow fleet' tanker and oil depot at Taganrog and refineries as far as Volgograd and Krasnodar — while Russia fired about 90 drones and two ballistic missiles back; Moscow claimed a drone hit the Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant, which Kyiv flatly denied as 'propaganda'. In Singapore, the Shangri-La Dialogue wrapped up on a calmer note: China visibly toned down its rhetoric and US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth struck a milder line on Beijing, both following the recent Xi–Trump summit. In south Lebanon, Israeli troops still hold their deepest positions since 2006 on the edge of Nabatieh after pushing beyond the Litani River.

stories.headline: Ukraine hits Russian oil 'shadow fleet' tanker and refineries in deep overnight drone strikes
stories.summary: Ukraine said its drones struck Russian energy targets across several regions overnight on May 30 — a 'shadow fleet' oil tanker, a fuel tank and the port at Taganrog (Rostov), an oil facility at Armavir (Krasnodar) roughly 500km from the border, and refineries at Volgograd and Yaroslavl. Russian authorities said two civilians were injured at Taganrog and that fires were put out with no oil spill. Russia launched about 90 drones and two ballistic missiles at Ukraine in return, damaging the Sumy region. Russia's Rosatom claimed a drone hit a turbine hall at the Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant; Ukraine's military denied striking it, calling the claim 'propaganda'. (Ukrainian and Russian accounts; nuclear-plant claim disputed.)

stories.headline: Shangri-La Dialogue ends with China toning down rhetoric as US, Beijing both soften
stories.summary: The 23rd Shangri-La Dialogue in Singapore closed with a notable de-escalation in tone. The head of China's delegation, Major General Meng Xiangqing, warned of 'hegemonism' and 'bloc confrontation' by unnamed countries but avoided the sharp US criticism seen last year, and said Beijing hopes military ties can develop on a 'healthy, stable and sustainable path'. The shift mirrored US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth's milder line on China and is widely tied to the recent Xi–Trump summit in Beijing. Hegseth had earlier urged Asian allies to spend more on defence and said the US was 'more than capable' of resuming strikes on Iran if talks fail.
stories.source: South China Morning Post / Bloomberg / Al Jazeera

stories.headline: Israeli forces hold deepest positions in south Lebanon at the edge of Nabatieh
stories.summary: Israeli troops remain dug in beyond the Litani River on the outskirts of Nabatieh — their deepest advance into south Lebanon since 2006 — after fighting Hezbollah in nearby villages such as Zawtar al-Sharqiya. Israeli and Lebanese officers held their first security talks since April at the Pentagon. Lebanon's army reported an Israeli drone seriously wounded two of its soldiers near Nabatieh; Hezbollah said it fired rockets and drones at the advancing forces. Lebanon's Health Ministry puts the war's toll above 3,200 killed. (Israeli advance; Lebanese-reported casualties.)
stories.source: Al Jazeera / CBC News / Washington Times (AP)

singapore.label: Singapore
singapore.ai_summary: It's the closing day of the Shangri-La Dialogue, which Singapore hosted (May 29–31). Defence Minister Chan Chun Sing addressed a plenary on security partnerships 'in a fragmenting world', and the forum ended on a calmer note as both the US and China softened their tone. Singapore was also busy on the diplomatic front: it wrapped up a state visit by Vietnam's President To Lam, with PM Wong and Lam planting a tree at the Botanic Gardens and signing a raft of agreements including a new Singapore–Vietnam Strategic Dialogue. Weather-wise, expect a cloudy, warm afternoon (high around 34°C) with a high chance of showers.

stories.headline: Shangri-La Dialogue wraps up in Singapore; Chan Chun Sing speaks on partnerships in a 'fragmenting world'
stories.summary: The 23rd IISS Shangri-La Dialogue concluded today after Singapore hosted 44 countries and 54 ministerial-level delegates. Defence Minister Chan Chun Sing addressed a plenary on 'Evolving Security Partnerships in a Fragmenting World' and hosted Five Power Defence Arrangements ministers. The forum ended on a calmer note than recent years, with China's delegation toning down its rhetoric and the US striking a milder line on Beijing.
stories.source: Singapore Ministry of Defence (MINDEF) / IISS / SCMP

stories.headline: Singapore concludes Vietnam President To Lam's state visit with new Strategic Dialogue, MOUs
stories.summary: Singapore wrapped up the May 28–31 state visit of Vietnam's Communist Party General Secretary and President To Lam. PM Lawrence Wong and President Lam planted a Hopea recopei tree at the Singapore Botanic Gardens on May 30 to mark the relationship and witnessed several agreements, including the establishment of a Singapore–Vietnam Strategic Dialogue and MOUs on supply-chain resilience, agrifood, advanced manufacturing and a specialised international commercial court within Vietnam's International Finance Centre.

stories.headline: Singapore and 16 other countries launch GUIDE pact to protect undersea infrastructure
stories.summary: On the sidelines of the Shangri-La Dialogue, 17 countries — including Singapore, Malaysia and Australia — launched the Guiding Principles for Underwater Infrastructure Defence Exchanges (GUIDE), an effort to safeguard critical undersea cables and pipelines through information-sharing and coordination amid rising concern over sabotage threats.

stories.headline: Cloudy, warm afternoon with a high chance of showers
stories.summary: NEA's Meteorological Service 24-hour forecast (updated 2:51pm, May 31) is for cloudy conditions with a 60–95% chance of rain, temperatures ranging from about 25°C to a high near 34°C, and southerly winds of 10–20 km/h. A forecast of thundery showers carries an imminent risk of lightning; readers should seek shelter if storms develop.

wars.label: Wars & Conflicts
wars.ai_summary: All three theatres remain active. In Ukraine, Kyiv escalated its deep-strike campaign — overnight on May 30 it hit a Russian 'shadow fleet' tanker and oil depots at Taganrog plus refineries as far as Volgograd, while Russia fired about 90 drones and two missiles back and claimed (disputed by Kyiv) a drone struck the Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant. In the Gulf, ceasefire-extension talks remain unfinished — Hegseth said the US is 'more than capable' of resuming strikes on Iran while Tehran pushes back, and the Strait of Hormuz stays effectively closed. In Lebanon, Israel still holds its deepest positions since 2006 on the edge of Nabatieh after crossing the Litani; the sides met at the Pentagon.

theatres.name: Russia-Ukraine War
theatres.status: Kyiv presses deep strikes on Russian oil; both sides trade drone and missile attacks; nuclear-plant strike claim disputed

timeline.event: One of Ukraine's largest drone strikes on Russian territory kills at least four people, including three near Moscow, and wounds about a dozen, local authorities say.

Ukraine says drone strikes killed at least 65 Russian drone-pilot cadets and an instructor at a training camp in occupied territory; Zelensky says a separate strike killed and wounded nearly 100. (Ukrainian claims; Russia had not confirmed tolls.)

Russia hits Kyiv with its largest attack on the capital of the war — Ukraine's Air Force says about 600 drones and 90 missiles were launched, including a hypersonic Oreshnik that struck Bila Tserkva; defences downed or jammed most. Kyiv officials report several killed and dozens injured. (Russia did not dispute the strike.)
A Russian drone crashes into an apartment building in Galati, eastern Romania, wounding two — Romania summons Moscow's ambassador and NATO/EU condemn the incursion, raising spillover concerns.

Ukraine strikes deep inside Russia overnight — a 'shadow fleet' oil tanker and depot at Taganrog, an oil facility at Armavir (~500km from the border), and refineries at Volgograd and Yaroslavl; Russian officials report two civilians injured at Taganrog. Russia fires about 90 drones and two ballistic missiles at Ukraine, damaging Sumy. Russia's Rosatom claims a drone hit a turbine hall at the Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant; Ukraine denies striking it, calling it 'propaganda'. (Both sides' accounts; nuclear-plant claim disputed. Latest state of play.)

theatres.name: US/Israel-Iran (Strait of Hormuz)
theatres.status: Ceasefire-extension talks unfinished; US threatens to resume strikes, Iran pushes back; strait still effectively closed

timeline.event: US and Israel launch a coordinated air war on Iran, striking military, nuclear and leadership sites; Iran retaliates with missiles and drones on Israel, US bases and Gulf states.
Iran declares the Strait of Hormuz 'closed' and threatens to attack ships transiting it; the IRGC boards merchant vessels and lays sea mines, choking the key oil chokepoint.
Trump says a deal to reopen the Strait of Hormuz is 'largely negotiated' and will be announced soon.

US and Iranian negotiators reach a tentative 60-day framework to extend the ceasefire, reopen and de-mine the Strait of Hormuz and begin nuclear talks — but it awaits Trump's final approval and Iran has not formally confirmed; the strait remains effectively closed.

The heads of the IEA, IMF, World Bank and WTO jointly warn that global oil inventories are being drawn down 'at a record pace' due to the loss of supply through Hormuz, with growing risks to fuel security ahead of peak summer demand.

At the Shangri-La Dialogue, Hegseth says the US is 'more than capable' of resuming strikes on Iran if talks fail; an adviser to Iran's Supreme Leader accuses Trump of 'betraying diplomacy', underscoring that the framework is not finalised. The strait remains effectively closed. (Latest state of play.)

theatres.name: Israel-Lebanon / Gaza
theatres.status: Israel holds positions beyond the Litani at the edge of Nabatieh (since May 30); sides met at the Pentagon, no new advance confirmed since

timeline.event: An October 2025 ceasefire with Hamas takes effect in Gaza, with Israel permitted to temporarily hold roughly half the territory.
Israel and Lebanon agree a US-brokered ceasefire (effective ~April 17), initially a short truce to halt fighting and open negotiations.

Israeli air attacks across southern Lebanon and near the Syrian border kill at least 20 people, Lebanon's Health Ministry says, despite the ceasefire; Israel says it is targeting Hezbollah. (Lebanese toll; Israel cites militant targets.)

Netanyahu says he ordered the IDF to seize 70% of Gaza — beyond the truce terms — saying Israel already controls about 60%; the UN warns it would deepen the humanitarian crisis. In Lebanon, Israel strikes a southern Beirut suburb and southern towns. (Lebanese-reported tolls.)

Netanyahu says Israeli forces crossed the Litani River and seized commanding terrain in southern Lebanon, vowing to crush Hezbollah; Lebanese authorities report new deadly strikes. (Israeli claim; Lebanese-reported casualties.)

Israeli troops reach the outskirts of Nabatieh — their deepest push into south Lebanon since 2006 — fighting Hezbollah in nearby villages, as Israeli and Lebanese officers hold security talks at the Pentagon. Lebanon's army says an Israeli drone seriously wounded two of its soldiers near Nabatieh; Hezbollah says it fired rockets and drones at the advancing forces. (Israeli advance; Lebanese-reported casualties. Latest state of play.)
timeline.source: Al Jazeera / Washington Times (AP) / NBC News

outbreaks.label: Outbreaks
outbreaks.ai_summary: The DRC/Uganda Bundibugyo Ebola epidemic — a WHO-declared PHEIC since May 17 — is still spreading faster than responders can contain it. The DRC reported 125 confirmed cases (17 deaths) as of May 28, plus 906 suspected cases (223 suspected deaths) as of May 29; Uganda has 7–9 confirmed (agency tallies differ) and one death, and has closed a high-traffic border crossing. There is no licensed vaccine or specific treatment for the Bundibugyo strain. Separately, the MV Hondius Andes-hantavirus cluster holds steady at 13 cases and 3 deaths, with the ship disembarked in Rotterdam and no confirmed onward spread. Public risk outside affected settings remains low.

outbreaks.name: DRC / Uganda — Bundibugyo Ebola (PHEIC)
outbreaks.status: PHEIC since 17 May; UN warns spread is outpacing containment
outbreaks.scope_note: Lab-confirmed only, DRC + Uganda combined: DRC 125 confirmed / 17 confirmed deaths (DRC Ministry of Health, as of 28 May); Uganda 7 (WHO) to 9 (ECDC) confirmed, 1 death — agency tallies diverge, at least 3 Uganda cases imported from DRC. WHO separately reports 906 suspected cases / 223 suspected deaths in DRC as of 29 May. No licensed vaccine or specific therapeutic exists for the Bundibugyo strain.

outbreaks.name: MV Hondius — Andes hantavirus
outbreaks.status: Limited person-to-person spread aboard ship; off-ship risk low
outbreaks.scope_note: 13 cases = 11 lab-confirmed Andes virus (ANDV) + 2 probable; 3 deaths (WHO Disease Outbreak News, latest update late May 2026, corroborated by ECDC). All cases are MV Hondius passengers/crew from a single vessel; the '32' is the number of countries/territories tracing 600+ contacts, NOT countries with cases. The ship disembarked in Rotterdam on 18 May; its 23 crew entered quarantine there. No confirmed onward community transmission off the ship.

stories.headline: UN warns DRC/Uganda Ebola epidemic is spreading faster than containment
stories.summary: The UN and WHO warned that the Bundibugyo Ebola epidemic in eastern DRC is outpacing the response. As of 29 May, WHO reported 906 suspected cases (223 suspected deaths) in the DRC alongside 125 confirmed cases and 17 confirmed deaths in Ituri, North Kivu and South Kivu. Uganda has reported 7 confirmed cases (three imported from DRC) and one death. Insecurity in the affected provinces is hampering containment; WHO declared the epidemic a PHEIC on 17 May.
stories.source: UN News / WHO Disease Outbreak News

stories.headline: Uganda closes a high-traffic border crossing as rare Ebola strain spreads
stories.summary: Uganda closed a high-traffic border crossing with the DRC to slow the spread of the rare Bundibugyo Ebola strain after a rise in cases, several of which were linked to travel from the DRC. The Bundibugyo virus is uniquely challenging because, unlike Zaire ebolavirus, it has no licensed vaccine or approved specific therapeutic; care is supportive.

stories.headline: MV Hondius hantavirus cluster steady at 13 cases, 3 deaths; ship disembarked in Rotterdam
stories.summary: WHO's latest update puts the MV Hondius Andes-hantavirus cluster at 13 cases (11 confirmed, 2 probable) and 3 deaths, all among passengers and crew of the single vessel. The ship reached Rotterdam on 18 May and its 23 crew entered quarantine; more than 600 contacts are being monitored across 32 countries, with no confirmed onward community transmission off the ship.

cybersecurity.label: CyberSecurity
cybersecurity.ai_summary: Edge and infrastructure flaws still lead this week. An actively-exploited authentication-bypass in Palo Alto's GlobalProtect VPN (CVE-2026-0257) and a newly disclosed Linux kernel 'CIFSwitch' local-root bug both landed May 30 — the Palo Alto flaw is in CISA's KEV. A critical FortiClient EMS flaw (CVE-2026-35616, CVSS 9.1) is being abused to push an 'EKZ' infostealer disguised as a Fortinet patch. On the breach front, the ShinyHunters gang leaked 4.9 million Charter Communications customer records, attackers abused ChatGPT share links to deliver malware, and Dutch police dismantled a 17-million-device residential-proxy botnet.

stories.headline: Palo Alto GlobalProtect VPN auth-bypass (CVE-2026-0257) now exploited in attacks
stories.summary: Palo Alto Networks confirmed limited active exploitation of an authentication-bypass in PAN-OS GlobalProtect that lets attackers forge override cookies to log in as the local admin; Rapid7 traced successful exploitation to mid-May and urges treating it as critical despite the 7.8 score. CISA added it to the KEV catalog. Affected configs use GlobalProtect with authentication override cookies enabled.

stories.headline: New 'CIFSwitch' Linux kernel flaw gives local root on multiple distributions
stories.summary: Researchers disclosed CIFSwitch, a local-privilege-escalation bug in the Linux kernel's CIFS subsystem that fails to verify the origin of cifs.spnego key requests, letting an unprivileged user forge one and load a malicious NSS module to gain root. Present since 2007, it affects stock builds of distributions including Linux Mint, CentOS Stream 9, Rocky/AlmaLinux 9 and SLES 15; a kernel patch validates request origins.

stories.headline: ShinyHunters leak 4.9 million Charter Communications customer records
stories.summary: The ShinyHunters extortion gang leaked data on 4.9 million Charter Communications accounts after an April vishing attack compromised an employee's Microsoft Entra account and the firm's Salesforce instance. Exposed data includes names, emails, addresses, phone numbers and support-ticket details; Charter says no sensitive personal information or CPNI was taken.

stories.headline: Attackers abuse ChatGPT share links to host fake outage pages that deliver malware
stories.summary: Threat actors are abusing OpenAI's ChatGPT conversation-sharing feature to host convincing fake 'service outage' pages on a trusted chatgpt.com domain, luring victims into downloading malware. The technique borrows the credibility of a legitimate AI platform to slip past user suspicion and some filters.

stories.headline: Dutch police dismantle 17-million-device 'Asocks' residential-proxy botnet
stories.summary: The Hague police cybercrime unit and the Dutch NCSC took down the Asocks botnet — at least 17 million compromised consumer devices worldwide run via about 200 Netherlands-based servers — which had been sold as a 'residential proxy' service to route malicious traffic and launch attacks. The servers were seized and the botnet taken offline.

stories.headline: Critical FortiClient EMS flaw (CVE-2026-35616) abused to drop 'EKZ' infostealer disguised as a Fortinet patch
stories.summary: Arctic Wolf reported a campaign abusing the critical FortiClient EMS access-control flaw (CVE-2026-35616) — already in CISA's KEV since April — to deploy an undocumented credential stealer dubbed 'EKZ'. The malware is disguised as a legitimate Fortinet endpoint patch and steals browser credentials, cookies and payment data from Chromium and Firefox. Fortinet shipped emergency hotfixes; Shadowserver reported roughly 2,000 internet-exposed instances.
